content.title: Dealer Tire — Designing a 5-Step Purchase Journey
content.summary: Dealer Tire powers tire purchasing for OEM dealerships nationwide. I redesigned the end-to-end purchase flow as one cohesive experience. Conversion increased 42%.

content.alt: A single wide image showing all 5 screens in a horizontal row inside browser/device frames. Above each screen, a connected progress bar — Step 1 through Step 5 — with each step labelled: "Vehicle & Location · Dealer Selection · Tire Results · Appointment · Complete Purchase." The screens look cohesive — same nav, same typography, same component style across all 5. This one image communicates the entire journey and the design thinking behind it instantly.
content.caption: Complete 5-step purchase journey

content.body: Dealer Tire powers tire purchasing for OEM dealerships nationwide. The existing online storefront had the right idea — a step-by-step purchase flow — but the experience felt like five disconnected pages built at different times. No consistent progress indicator, no continuity between steps, no sense of guided journey. Dealers were dropping off at 68%. I redesigned the end-to-end purchase flow as one experience. Conversion increased 42%.

The old flow had five steps but didn't feel like it. Each page looked slightly different. There was no persistent progress indicator telling dealers where they were or how close they were to done. Context from the previous step — the car they'd entered, the dealer they'd chosen — wasn't being carried forward visually. Every page felt like starting over. That friction compounded across five steps and dealers were abandoning.

content.alt: Three bold stat cards on a dark background, large white numbers, small grey labels beneath each. "68%" — cart abandonment across the full purchase flow. "Step 3" — the highest drop-off point, tire results page, where dealers lost context of their vehicle. "15 fields" — total form inputs spread across the journey with no smart defaults or pre-fill. Stark, factual, no decoration.
content.caption: Key research findings

content.body: I ran usability sessions with OEM dealers working through the full purchase flow. Three things kept surfacing. Dealers forgot what they entered by the time they reached tire results. They couldn't tell how many steps were left at any point in the journey. And switching between steps — going back to change a dealer selection for example — wiped progress and sent them back to step one. The experience had no memory and no map.

content.text: The Design Solution — One Journey, Five Steps

content.body: The central decision was to treat the five steps as a single guided journey rather than five separate pages. A persistent progress bar runs across the top of every screen. Selected context — vehicle, dealer, tires chosen — carries forward visually into each subsequent step so dealers always know what they've confirmed. Going back to a previous step preserves everything selected after it.

content.alt: Desktop browser screen. Clean centred layout, Dealer Tire nav at top. Large friendly headline: "Let's find the right tires for your car." Below it three dropdown fields in a row — Year / Make / Model — and a location input field with option alongside it. A single primary button beneath: "Find Dealers Near Me." Progress bar at the top shows Step 1 active, steps 2 through 5 greyed out. The page has one job and nothing else on it. Feels like a starting line, not a form.
content.caption: Step 1: Entry point

content.body: This is the entry point and it sets the tone for everything that follows. The vehicle and location entered here persist as a confirmation strip across every subsequent step — a small bar beneath the nav that reads "2022 Toyota Camry · Columbus, OH" — so dealers never lose context.

content.alt: Desktop screen. The confirmation strip is visible beneath the nav showing the vehicle and location from Step 1. Below it a clean list of dealership results — each result is a card showing dealership name, distance, address, available appointment indicator in green ("Next available: Tomorrow"), and a "Select This Dealer" button. Three results visible, clean spacing between each. Progress bar shows Step 2 active, Step 1 showing a green checkmark. Map on right showing dealer pins. Layout feels like a considered results page, not a raw data dump.
content.caption: Step 2: Dealer selection

content.alt: Desktop screen. Confirmation strip now shows vehicle and selected dealer. Main content is a product grid — tire cards showing tire photo, brand, model name, price, and a small green tag reading "Fits your 2022 Toyota Camry." Filter bar across the top — Brand, Price, Type. Progress bar shows Steps 1 and 2 complete, Step 3 active. Each card has one CTA: "Select Tires." The fitment confirmation tag on every card is the key detail here — it removes the doubt of "will this actually fit my car" which was the biggest drop-off cause at this step.
content.caption: Step 3: Tire selection

content.alt: Desktop screen. Confirmation strip now shows vehicle, dealer, and selected tire. Main content is a clean appointment scheduler — a monthly calendar on the left with available dates highlighted, a time slot grid on the right showing morning and afternoon slots for the selected date. Available slots available in light grey, selected slot highlighted in brand colour. Below the time grid a simple dropdown for appointment type — Drop Off / Wait / Shuttle. Progress bar shows Steps 1 through 3 complete, Step 4 active. "Confirm Appointment Time" button at the bottom. Feels calm and organised.
content.caption: Step 4: Scheduling

content.body: This step was unique to Dealer Tire — most tire e-commerce doesn't include scheduling. The design challenge was making it feel like a natural part of the journey rather than a jarring detour. Keeping the confirmation strip visible and the progress bar consistent made the transition feel seamless.

content.alt: Desktop screen. Confirmation strip across the top showing all confirmed selections — vehicle, dealer, tire, appointment time. Main content split into two columns. Left column: full order summary — tire image, name, price, installation fee, appointment date and time, total. Right column: payment form — four fields only, card number, expiry, CVV, cardholder name. SSL lock badge and PCI logo placed directly above the card fields. Biometric payment option visible on mobile version shown as a smaller inset. Progress bar shows all steps complete except Step 5 which is active. Single button: "Complete Order." The full confirmation strip at the top means dealers can review everything they've selected in one glance before committing.
content.caption: Step 5: Purchase completion

content.text: The Progress Bar — Why It Mattered

content.alt: A close-up strip showing just the progress bar component in five states. One row per step — Step 1 active through Step 5 active. Completed steps show a checkmark and filled circle in brand colour. Active step shows filled circle with step label bold. Upcoming steps show empty grey circles with lighter labels. Clean, simple, consistent. The component that tied the whole experience together.
content.caption: Progress bar across all steps

content.body: This was the single highest-impact design decision. Before the redesign, dealers had no map. They didn't know if they were halfway through or one step from done. Adding persistent progress indicators — with completed steps showing checkmarks and carrying forward context — reduced drop-off at every step. Dealers knew where they were, what they've confirmed, and how close they were to finished.

content.alt: Five bold metric cards on a deep navy background. Large numbers, before/after in small grey beneath each. "+42% Conversion Rate" (2.8% → 4.0%) / "↓38% Cart Abandonment" (68% → 42%) / "↓54% Drop-off at Step 3" — the tire results page where context was being lost / "+55% Form Completion" / "4.6/5.0 Dealer Satisfaction" up from 3.2.
content.caption: Impact metrics

items: The progress bar changed behaviour before any individual step was redesigned. Visibility of journey position alone reduced abandonment. That's how much the absence of structure was costing conversions.

Carrying confirmed context forward — showing the vehicle, dealer, and tire selections as a persistent strip on every screen — was the fix for Step 3 drop-off. Dealers weren't confused by the tires. They were confused because they'd lost track of what they were shopping for.

The appointment step felt like the riskiest addition. In testing it became the most appreciated — dealers valued knowing their installation was booked as part of the same flow rather than a separate process.
